entries.end_notes: That words for the "good thing" can mean rain in Northwest Semitic was apparently first suggested by M. Dahood in his Anchor Bible Commentary volume on Psalms (II: 283). Subsequent studies by others such as Loretz (UF 21[1989]) and Rendsburg (VT 33[1983]) have augmented the evidence. The Aramaic evidence, though would appear to be limited to literal biblical translation.

In general prḥ is simply to fly like a simple bird while ṭws seems to be to fly around and swoop like a bird of prey; ṭwr, not used in the peal in finite forms, seems to have the connotation of flying upward.
The pael forms in Mandaic magic texts might be better interpreted as from ṭss, i.e. "blocked with plates" or the like.

Greek τύπος.
Given the many early traditions that the sin of Baal-Peor involved sexual practices, our rendering of the LJLA usage in this context is undoubtedly insufficient. Whatever the Moabite women were displaying beneath their skirts ended up seducing the Israelites. Perhaps we are dealing here rather with a euphemism for vagina (i.e., "mold"), thus טופסיה דפעור is intended to mean something like "wide open vagina".

entries.end_notes:
To judge by etymology, "young lamb" is the original meaning of this word (compare American "kid"), but that usage has almost totally disappeared from Aramaic other than in Jewish texts where all the examples can easily be taken to be Hebraisms. The scarcity of the meaning "lamb" in Aramaic was pointed out to us by Prof. Ed Cook.
Note from the examples that the age range of such youths normally can be anywhere from infancy to older teen-age, but for a substantial outlier (30-40 years!) see Am7 110:11.

The JBA passage cited as the sole Dt example may easily be interpreted as D instead as in many mss.
